handoff.summary: Retrospective completed. Documented scope delivery (9 prompts, 7 complete, 1 skipped, 1 retro), bootstrapping exception assessment (acceptable for self-referential protocol work), friction points (zeroed execution metrics, multi-agent handoff overhead), and 5 action items for future workpacks.
next_steps: Apply action items to next workpack planning cycle
known_issues: Execution metrics (tokens_in, tokens_out, duration_ms) are zeroed in all output JSONs — no LLM cost tracking was captured
Post-merge tooling work (validate_workpack_files.py, scaffold integration, manual prompts, WARN_MISSING_RETROSPECTIVE) emerged as unplanned scope

notes: Retrospective prompt — no source code changes. This output documents execution outcomes, process assessment, and action items. The retrospective itself is embedded in the handoff.summary and the notes field below.

1_scope_and_delivery_summary.description: This workpack delivered three complementary enhancements to the Workpack Protocol prompt lifecycle targeting version 2.2.0: (1) B-series dependency DAG support, (2) per-prompt commit tracking, and (3) legacy-to-modern workpack modernization with maintenance prompts. All 16 acceptance criteria from the request were satisfied.

agents_used.claude_sonnet_4_6: B1 (changelog), B2 review (skipped), A5 re-review (merge authorization)
agents_used.claude_opus_4_6: R1 (retrospective), post-merge tooling (validator, scaffold integration, docs)

delivery_timeline.notes: Post-merge tooling and retrospective work extended the total timeline significantly beyond merge authorization.

key_commits: 70434b2 — batch delivery of all A-series and B1 prompts
2623674 — SHA backfill and bootstrapping exception documentation

files_touched.total_modified: ~40 files across protocol spec, schemas, templates, linter, tests, tools, docs

2_what_went_well: Clean DAG execution: A1 and A2 ran in parallel as planned; dependency ordering was respected throughout.
Protocol backward compatibility preserved: existing 2.0.0/2.1.0 workpacks still lint and parse without modification.
B-series mechanism validated by practice: A5 correctly identified two blocking issues and generated B1+B2 with an explicit DAG, proving the feature works.
Multi-agent handoff succeeded: codex delivered A0–A5, then claude-sonnet-4-6 picked up B1 and the A5 re-review seamlessly.
All 16 acceptance criteria verified and documented with explicit pass/fail evidence in A5 output.
Post-merge tooling improvements (validate_workpack_files.py, scaffold integration, manual prompts) emerged organically and strengthened the protocol ecosystem.
Linter test coverage expanded: 16 → 27 tests across the session, all passing.
3_what_caused_friction: Bootstrapping paradox: A2 introduced commit tracking, but the protocol for commit tracking didn't exist yet when A0–A5 were being executed. This forced a retroactive SHA backfill and a documented exception — adding ~30 minutes of rework.

Zeroed execution metrics: No LLM cost tracking was captured in any output JSON (tokens_in=0, tokens_out=0, duration_ms=0 across all 7 outputs). This eliminates any possibility of cost analysis or token efficiency comparison.

Schema violations in initial state updates: workpack.state.json used 'superseded' (not in enum) and 'notes' (not in schema). These required manual fixes — the state schema's additionalProperties:false is strict and the error surfaced late.

Missing A3 output timing: A4 execution noted that A3 output was not yet present at the time. The execution log shows A3 was backfilled post-hoc at 00:25 UTC, suggesting the codex agent may not have produced it during the original A3 execution window.
R1 retrospective prompt was missing: the plan referenced R1_retrospective in Phase 6 but the prompt file was never created during the A-series work. It had to be created manually after the fact.
Multi-agent context loss: switching between codex and claude-sonnet-4-6 required re-establishing full context each time, adding overhead to the B-series and re-review phases.

B1_changelog_2_2_0.reason: AC11 failed — missing protocol 2.2.0 changelog entry
B1_changelog_2_2_0.resolution: Added 46-line [2.2.0] section to CHANGELOG.md covering all three features
B1_changelog_2_2_0.assessment: Legitimate gap — the codex agent focused on spec/schema/template/linter but did not maintain the changelog. This is a common oversight in protocol evolution workpacks.

B2_self_commit_audit_alignment.reason: Commit verification failed — A0–A4 outputs had empty commit_shas
B2_self_commit_audit_alignment.resolution: Skipped — bootstrapping exception documented. SHA 70434b2 backfilled retroactively.
B2_self_commit_audit_alignment.rework_cost: ~25 minutes for exception analysis and SHA backfill (01:00–01:15 UTC)
B2_self_commit_audit_alignment.assessment: Not a true defect — a structural impossibility. Commit tracking cannot be enforced before it exists.

4_defects_and_rework_analysis.total_rework_estimate: ~68 minutes of unplanned work across the session

planned_effort_table.assessment: Over-estimated — schema and spec changes were straightforward

5_estimation_accuracy.overall_assessment: A-series effort estimates were consistently over-estimated by 3–6x. The M-sized tasks (A1, A2, A4) completed in under 30 minutes each. This suggests that for protocol evolution work with clear specs, effort should be estimated one size smaller (M→S, S→XS). However, the unplanned rework (bootstrapping exception, schema fixes, tooling additions) consumed roughly as much time as the planned work itself.

6_bootstrapping_exception_assessment.situation: A2 introduced per-prompt commit tracking as a new protocol requirement. However, since all A-series prompts were delivered in a single batch by the codex agent, there was no opportunity to commit each prompt's changes individually before writing output.json. The commit tracking protocol did not exist when A0–A1 were executed.
6_bootstrapping_exception_assessment.decision: Document this as a bootstrapping exception. All 7 outputs share batch commit SHA 70434b2. B2 (self-commit audit) was skipped rather than artificially attempting to retroactively create per-prompt commits.
6_bootstrapping_exception_assessment.assessment: ACCEPTABLE. This is an inherent limitation of self-referential protocol work: you cannot enforce a rule that you are in the process of creating. The exception was properly documented in every output JSON with explanatory notes.
recommendations: Future workpacks that introduce new protocol requirements SHOULD plan for the bootstrapping case explicitly in the request/plan.
Consider a 'protocol bootstrap mode' flag in workpack.meta.json that relaxes newly-introduced requirements for the workpack that introduces them.
Self-referential protocol changes should be called out in the risk matrix with the bootstrapping paradox as a known risk.

7_action_items.action: Add execution metrics collection guidance to PROTOCOL_SPEC.md
7_action_items.rationale: All 7 outputs had zeroed tokens/duration — no cost analysis possible

7_action_items.action: Add bootstrapping exception pattern to PROTOCOL_SPEC.md risk guidance
7_action_items.rationale: Self-referential protocol changes will recur; document the pattern once

7_action_items.action: Reduce default effort estimates for protocol evolution tasks by one T-shirt size
7_action_items.rationale: A1/A2/A4 planned as M, completed in <30m each; systematic over-estimation

7_action_items.action: Include R1_retrospective prompt in scaffold template
7_action_items.rationale: R1 was missing from the initial workpack and had to be created manually

7_action_items.action: Ensure changelog update is part of A-series, not deferred to B-series
7_action_items.rationale: B1 was generated solely because A-series didn't maintain the changelog — avoidable rework
